sentences.text: My name is Vera Laska.
And what else?
L-A-S-K-A.
My first name is Vera, V-E-R-A.
[SIGHS] Oravec, O-R-A-V-E-C.
I was born in 1928.
1st--21st of July.
Oh, don't ask nasty questions.
Figure it out. 1928-- 78-- So I must be getting close to 70.
I was born in Kosice, Czechoslovakia.
K-O-S-I-C-E.

Well, I grew up in a very interesting neighborhood, because it was an ethnic mixture, which gave me, now that I'm looking back, gave me some kind of a good background to what was to follow in my career in the United States, because my family at home spoke Czech.
The neighborhood was Slovak.
The school was Slovak, although there were many Czech teachers.
And there was quite a bit of a minority among-- among us of Germans and Hungarians.
Most kids.
You know, in that Central European milieu, most people spoke several languages, not like in the United States where we have a continent that speaks one language, English.
But there, it's sort of mixed up.
The countries are so small that if you start jogging, you jog out of your country.
You jog out of your language.
So by necessity you had to learn languages.
And we had-- in high school, we had several languages-- French, German, not mentioning Latin, which we had to take, which was very helpful in learning English later.
That is a very difficult question to answer because my mother came from a very well-to-do and highly respected family.
And when she was young, there was lots of money to go around.
While after she got married and divorced, there was no money.
So our home looked like a million dollars, with Persian rugs and what have you, but there was very little-- very little money.
[LAUGHS] I must have been quite a character, very strong-willed.
My poor mother couldn't manage me.
I always did what I thought is right, which wasn't necessarily always the-- the correct thing.
But I wanted to devote my life to sports.
I originally wanted to be a-- a professional athlete or swimmer or skier or something.
Luckily, I must have seen the light later on and switched to being-- dreaming of becoming a teacher, which I did.
No, not at all.
No, I was born Catholic, but not practicing.
Yes, we did, because some of the elite in the town were Jewish and highly respected, such as lawyers or doctors.
I remember our dentist was Jewish.
Our doctor was Jewish.
I don't remember lawyer business.
But I knew several kids in the swimming club or in school whose dad was-- whose fathers were lawyers.
And they were Jewish.
There was no prejudice.
I tell you one thing.
One of my closest friends as a teenager was a girl who happened to be Jewish, which I didn't know for years.
For years I didn't know until this Hitler business started, and she made some comments.
And I said, what's that to you?
And she said, well I'm Jewish, stupid.
And I said, I didn't know.
How should I know?
So we grew up in a very happy [NON-ENGLISH] so to speak, mostly perhaps because the creator of the country, Masaryk-- M-A-S-A-R-Y-K-- was a philosopher and concentrated on ethics.
And he was a humanitarian.
And there was no way that we were taught being anti-Semitic or anti anything.
Did your family have any political affiliations?
No.
Well, we were discussing it, but I never was a joiner of any political party-- not as a kid, not as a teenager, down to our days.
I-- I am not a card-carrying anything of any political party.
I'm trying to do what is morally right.
You can put me into a category of humanitarian or something like that, but definitely no political party.
Nowadays if I vote, I vote for the person, whether he's Republican or a Democrat.
Not really.
I read a lot.
And I hate to repeat this business of President Masaryk, but I read much earlier than I should have, perhaps, all these works about what the meaning of life is and how people should behave.
And that was-- if there was a shining star for me to follow, it was he and his-- his disciples.
Just about nothing.
Just about nothing.
Strange, isn't it?
I see the building of the school.
But I would have trouble remembering the names of teachers.
Oh, I remember one thing.
I remember one thing.
They almost threw me out of first grade.
I'm very proud of that [LAUGHS] because the reason was I kept asking and why, and where, and who.
And so the teacher called in my mom and said, if that child will not shut up and ask those impertinent questions, we'll send her to reform school.
And my mother cried.
That was her reaction.
And today, of course, as we look at it, I must have a smart cookie.
I wanted to know.
But the teacher disagreed.
So that's what I remember, first grade.
No.
About sports?
Oh, you have hard time stopping me.
Well, from childhood on I loved water and swimming.
So I became a member very early-- in the child category still-- member of the Czech sports club, swimming division.
And I spent every possible hour in the summer-- didn't have winter pools at that time-- at the swimming pool, swimming, training, winning races-- never lost a race- and just socializing.
It was a wonderful period of growing up.
In the winter, I had a different kind of a group, and we went skiing.
And in between, we had-- we went kayaking on the river.
And to see Czechoslovakia, which is called the land of the Yankees of Europe, was very sports-minded and helped young people do sports.
We had all kinds of lower rates in-- not hotels.
What would we call-- the hostels for skiing and so on.
So there were special trains that took our bicycles down the river, over weekends only.
And we went down by-- kayak or canoe.
And coming back, again, we could put the kayak on the train, freight train, for peanuts really, and bicycle back home.
And this went on year by year, weekend by weekend.
But mostly we knew the trails from hiking because that leads into our subject, you know, of smuggling people across the border, and all that.
Did you have any particularly close friendships, either from the sporting groups or school?
I had many, many good friends.
And I mean good friends, not in the superficial sense, you know, you meet somebody and he's a friend.
I mean a friend who would take off his last shirt from his back or her back, and vice versa.
I had many good friends.
And little by little, I'm losing them, of course.
Oh yeah, my closest friend was a girl by the name of Magda, who lived in the next house where-- where I grew up.

And her parents took out a piece in the fence and made two little steps, one on each side, so we put commute without going on the street-- dangerous, because every half and hour there was a car, you know.
So-- and that friendship lasted very long.
As a matter of fact, she visited me in America.
We invited her.
She was in this house.
And unfortunately she's no longer with us.
She was my very closest friend.
Magda Kiss, K-I-S-S.
Well, what about it?
Again, we had lots of languages.
In the whole-- The system is something slightly different than the American system.
So what here would be middle school or junior high and high school is packed into one.
And we had no choice in subjects, which I still approve of.
That's what we need in our high schools in the States.
We had six years of Latin, four years of German, four years of French, no choice.
The only choice we had was typewriting, and I don't know what else, sort of almost like extracurricular activities.
But that was extra in the afternoon.
Otherwise, six days a week, eight to one.
And everything prescribed, so that we had a very thorough and good general education.
You know, I teach American history here at Regis College.
And we come to the frontier and frontier literature.
And there is more that I did not forget for my high school in Czechoslovakia than these kids ever heard of, which is very sad, very sad.
They don't know American literature.
Oh yes, public school.
It was an all girls school, yes.
Not that they would dovetail into our story, no.
Well, when the war began, in Europe, I was 12 or 13.
Well, I remember anger.
I saw-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] a few months before the war started, I saw the German army march into my country.
And it was heartbreaking.

It was very heartbreaking, because all these ideals that we grew up with-- freedom, truth shall prevail-- all that went down the drain, you know, first of all by the treacherous behavior of the-- of France and England, who sold Czechoslovakia down the river at Munich.
They sold out.
Treaty, no treaty, you know.
And so it was a very bitter moment.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] But life went on.
And we still went skiing.
And we still were swimming around.
But it was very sad.
Not really, because as I said before, I wasn't politically active.
And so I didn't give political speeches also.
And if we were cursing the Germans, we cursed the Germans in our closed company of friends, where just about everybody had the same feelings.

But you had to start to watch your mouth when you were in public, let's say in a coffee house, or at intermission in a movie, because you never knew who stands next to you and might report you that you said that Hitler is an idiot or some grandiose statement like that.

Most of them that I remember got sick to their stomachs, because evidently when they came to Czechoslovakia, they saw for the first time in a long time whip cream or-- or good chocolate or what have you.
And they just overate.
And the hospitals were full of German soldiers who had their stomachs pumped out, at which point, of course, we rejoiced.
And that leads me to mention a little episode during the war, what the resistance did-- put laxative in-- in the coffee of the Nazis.
And I though this was a Czech invention, which is far from the truth, because I talk to people in Greece and Norway and points in between.
They all did the same thing.
The human mind works, to a large degree, the same way, nationality or no nationality.
Very easy item on the list of resistors-- put laxatives in their coffee.
Unfortunately we didn't see the result, you know, how they were holding their tummies and running, you know.
But that happened all over the place.
I started being aware of that when they actively did something.
I didn't see, for instance-- well, I heard that in Germany, for instance, the-- the Jews could not be doctors or lawyers or professors, and so on.
But it was sort of like when you hear thunder far in the background.
I was too young to care, probably.
And you know, the town where I was was given to Hungary, as at Munich the Sudetenland was given to the Germans.
And so the Hungarians said, and we want the southern part of Slovakia.
So Kosice went Hungarian.
And the Hungarian Jews were relatively very lucky, because trouble-- real serious trouble for life in Hungary didn't start until Eichmann came to Hungary on March 19.
That's Saint Joseph's Day.
That's how I remember the date.
And he started the ball rolling in Hungary.
By that time I was long gone.

You know, I sort of slid into it willy nilly via sports, in a way, because a friend of mine was approached by the resistance, which was leading or organizing the Underground Railroad from Slovakia to Hungary, from Hungary to Yugoslavia, and so on.
And she was approached and said hey, there are some French prisoners of war who escaped-- they didn't even say that-- just two Frenchmen who want to get to Hungary, but they don't have papers.
Would you will lead them across the mountains?
Do you think you could cross the border and the mountains so nobody would see?
And my friend said, well maybe, maybe, if a friend of mine could come too.
I don't want to do it alone.
And she asked me.
And I said sure, why not?
Why not?
But it wasn't like, this is the resistance, sign here, you know.
So very casually-- and after we did that, and it was successful, they said, well, would you do it again?
Sure, we will do it again.
So then we realized hey, we are in the resistance.
It was-- Maybe I shouldn't admit that, but that's how it happened, you know.
Not everybody was rah, rah, I'm going to join the resistance.
And then we stayed.
Now, that was our decision.
And from then on that we knew what we are doing, it's automatic, OK.
But how we got into it is sort of-- it was very casual.
And then, of course, once we were in this enterprise of taking the-- we called them Pierre.
Every Frenchman was Pierre.
We took the Pierres over, which wasn't too difficult.
I have a slightly funny description of that in my book, how we got them through.
We tried to teach them skiing.
It was hopeless.
So we created a gadget.
And we have that gadget in the United States, sold for good money.
It's a piece of metal.
And the child sits on it and slides down the hill.
Now what it that called?
I gave it-- I don't know whether your TV camera can stand it-- but-- but I translated it in my book as ass sled.
I don't want to spell that, but leave it to your imagination.
So that's how we got them downhill.
And then uphill, they had to pull this gimmick or gadget.
But then, of course, trouble started with the Jews in Slovakia.
And so the Jews started fleeing.
And evidently, somebody from among the Jews contacted the resistance leadership.
And so then they said well, no-- no Frenchmen this time, but there is a Jewish family.
Would you take them?
So we said, well, why not?
It makes no difference to us.
And I think we must have looked at ourselves like, wow, look what we are doing.
We are spiting Hitler.
We just based this on the resistance.
And so from then on, we started taking Jews here and there.
Another Frenchman got away from a camp in Poland.
But then we took Jews.
And that, again, is in my book description, what troubles we had with some of the people, passengers that we took.
Well I was a teenager.
Oh, definitely, yeah.
By whom?
No, I think that the people we were smuggling-- the Frenchmen were fun.
And they-- they didn't take their own fate seriously.
They were prisoners of war.
So that if they were caught, there were thrown back into camp.
That's it, you know.
And the Jews were so worried that they couldn't care who the devil is helping them, as long as we were helping them.
Some of them asked, well, how old are you, deary?
We didn't answer, or we said whatever.
What's you name?
Maria.
You didn't give names away to-- at least not real names, because the next hour they can be captured and interrogated.
And who brought you here?
So and so.
So we didn't give names.
Do you recall now the name of the friend who got you involved in the first place?
Yeah, yeah, Magda.
That's a different Magda.
It just sort happened that it was Magda too.
I forget her last name [INAUDIBLE].
[DRINKS]
Sometimes yes, sometimes no, because many times either there was too much snow or too little snow, whatever, so we stayed on the Hungarian side at some safe house.
But basically, yes.
Well, I'll take your question literally, and then the answer is no.
I found out after the war many, many other things that I didn't know at that time.

But I-- you could have figured that if we are doing-- saving people or smuggling people across the border in, you know, Poland, Slovakia, Hungary, Yugoslavia, that similar things must have been going on in the west, which they were, also underground railroads on so on.
I teach nowadays, So I know much more today-- much, much more today-- than I knew at that time.
There was no name.
Czechoslovakia it had to be, yeah.

There were so many such lines that-- maybe in France or in Belgium-- I know more today about details and names of the Belgian or French resistance, underground railroad, then I know about the east for a very simple reason.
Much more is written about it.
And if anything, don't forget that Czechoslovakia was under communists, and barely anything came out unless they wanted it to come out.
And they would enlarge or embellish certain aspects which had nothing to do with the Western allies for instance, only the Russians.
And even that-- I don't read Russian.
I mean, I could read one sentence in-- in five minutes.
And I won't bother with that.
So very little is today known about the eastern part of the resistance.
Oh, I should have prepared a map.
Well, you know Czechoslovak is like a sausage-- long country with a head.
I'm just thinking for one second if I have very handily a map.
No-- I-- Should I draw you a map, or it's not necessary?
OK.
Well anyhow, the tail end is Slovakia.
So it's narrowing.
And on top you have Poland.
Now, where they came from in Poland, I have no idea.
And then-- so evidently there must have been some Poles involved, when they got out for instance, the Pierres-- the Frenchmen-- when they got away from the-- from the prisoner of war camp.
And somebody got them through the border, which must have been very difficult, because it's highly mountainous, you know, like the Rockies, the Tatra Mountains.
And then they must have gotten to Presov or nearby.
And then somebody brought them across the border into Slovakia.
And from the Slovak border, they took them to where we picked them up.
We took them on the Slovak-Hungarian border.
In Hungary, somebody else picked them-- it's like a chain.
And you usually knew one person to contact, you know, or that person contacted you.
I'm sending you two packages.
One is a little bit torn, which means he limps, or he's sick or something.
And-- but I hope you get them by Saturday.
But I have to run now.
It's almost noon.
So I'll see you next time.
So we knew 12 o'clock Saturday.
And we knew where.
We were told before where, to pick them up at the railroad station or at the street corner, whatever.
And then we took them over.
And the same way our group notified the next guy that two package [LAUGHS] are on the way, or whatever-- no high secret writing or nothing like that.
It was rather primitive, honestly.
But it worked, most of the time.
